text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025

children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

22.1	The Economic and Social Commission for Western Asia (ESCWA) is responsible for promoting inclusive and sustainable development in the Arab region. The mandate derives from the priorities established in relevant General Assembly and Economic and Social Council resolutions and decisions, including Council resolution 1818 (LV) establishing the Commission and resolution 1985/69 amending its terms of reference to emphasize its social functions. The Commission’s membership includes 21 Arab member States.

22.2	The adoption of the Pact for the Future (see General Assembly resolution 79/1) is a crucial opportunity to redefine the trajectory of the Arab region, which, notwithstanding its potential and human capital, is still not on track for the implementation of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development and the achievement of all its Goals. The development gains achieved to date continue to be jeopardized by conflict, which, in some cases, has set back progress by decades; political instability and social unrest; governance challenges, social and economic challenges; and the impact of a changing climate on ecosystems. These developments are balanced by educated and highly motivated young people, abundant natural resources and innovative industrial investment. The Pact for the Future offers the region another chance to harness the transformative power of artificial intelligence, digital innovation and predictive analytics, in line with the Global Digital Compact, to address the complex and interrelated challenges that it faces, including economic volatility, social inequalities, conflicts and the threats of climate change; and to take decisive action towards achieving the aspirations of the people of the Arab region for a prosperous future for all. Given its regional mandate, ESCWA is uniquely positioned to support national development trajectories and priorities by leveraging its expertise, toolkits and partnerships, in support of jointly owned development solutions and outcomes.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

22.3	The Commission’s programme of work for 2027 remains guided by universal principles and rights, aligned with its mandates, and centred on tailored and timely support to member States in their efforts to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals. To deliver on this commitment, ESCWA will continue to provide evidence-based, novel policy options, underpinned by analysis made possible by its suite of interactive, data-driven modelling and policy simulation tools and knowledge repositories; to provide technical advice and enhance human and institutional capacities, including through peer-to-peer exchanges and the promotion of South-South and interregional collaboration; to leverage its convening role to promote inclusive dialogue, at the national and regional levels and forge consensus on viable development solutions; and to advocate for whole-of-government and whole-of-society approaches to accelerate progress against the Goals and their targets, while championing the region’s perspective through a global lens. The programme of work remains focused on high-impact interventions as a means of achieving inclusive and equitable development outcomes, which address the specific needs of groups in vulnerable situations, including women, young people, older persons and persons with disabilities; and the diverse and specific challenges of least developed and middle-income countries and countries in conflict, in line with the Doha Programme of Action for the Least Developed Countries for the Decade 2022–2031.

22.4	As defined by the objectives of the six subprogrammes, the Commission’s programme of work remains focused on areas such as climate action and the integrated management of water resources; the transition to new and renewable sources of energy, sustainable food systems and circular economy approaches; multidimensional poverty reduction and inclusive social protection; social and gender justice, women’s economic empowerment and improved access to labour markets, as part of the commitment to “leave no one behind”; macroeconomic, fiscal and trade policies, competitiveness of the business environment, and opportunities for small and medium-sized enterprises; improved access to innovative sources of development finance and sustainable debt management; information technology and statistics; the transparency and accessibility of public institutions, including through digitization; innovation and digital transformation; and governance, the assessment and mitigation of transboundary risks, including conflict, and boosting the effectiveness and efficiency of public administration.

22.5	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, ESCWA will sustain its strategic partnership with the League of Arab States and other intergovernmental organizations, as well as the Islamic Development Bank, the Arab Fund for Economic and Social Development and other relevant financial institutions. It will also promote South-South and triangular cooperation and further enhance its outreach to academic institutions, think tanks, civil society organizations and the private sector. New partnership opportunities will also be pursued to enable the Commission to continue to deliver support to member States, including the production of knowledge products and the convening of policymakers and other stakeholders.

22.6	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, as outlined in General Assembly resolution 72/279, the Commission will continue to discharge its function of co-Vice Chair of the Regional Collaborative Platform for the Arab States and its subsidiary issue-based coalitions. It will also work closely with the Development Coordination Office, resident coordinator offices, United Nations country teams, other regional commissions, the World Bank, the Department of Economic and Social Affairs and the United Nations Conference on Trade and Development to ensure that any support that it provides to member States is relevant, timely and effective and fully aligned with the commitments outlined within the national United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks, while avoiding the duplication of interventions and gaps.
22.7	With regard to external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:

(a)	Sufficient opportunities for protecting and leveraging development gains for further progress towards the implementation of the 2030 Agenda exist, notwithstanding the increasingly volatile global, regional and national dynamics and increased need for urgent coordinated humanitarian action and peacebuilding efforts;

(b)	Member States remain engaged and committed to collaborating on a range of sustainable development initiatives, with a specific focus on transboundary priorities and responses to emerging challenges;

(c)	Development partners remain committed to collaboration on and joint implementation of development initiatives, including their prioritization, identification of innovative solutions and opportunities for specific contributions;

(d)	The Commission’s operational preparedness and adaptive capacity enable it to maintain business continuity, even amid compromised safety and security emerging from conflict, political and social instability and/or natural disasters.

22.8	ESCWA integrates a gender perspective into its activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. This includes the provision of support to its member States in developing inclusive, gender-sensitive policy frameworks to address a wide range of economic and social issues and in enhancing the capacities of national institutions and actors, in addition to developing publications and technical materials specific to advancing gender equality and women’s economic empowerment in the region. The Commission’s commitment to gender equality remains in line with the United Nations global accountability framework and Economic and Social Council resolution 2023/11.

22.9	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, ESCWA will continue to make policy options available to member States and advocate in favour of the adoption of national frameworks that promote the greater inclusion and empowerment of persons with disabilities, including enhanced access to labour markets and basic social services.

22.10	ESCWA will integrate issues related to older persons and young people across its programme of work with the aim of augmenting their role as active contributors to the implementation of the 2030 Agenda. In this context, the Commission will: (a) promote their meaningful participation and inclusion in development dialogues; (b) generate and disseminate knowledge essential for developing policies that are responsive to the needs of young people and older persons; (c) facilitate peer-to-peer learning among member States within the region and beyond; and (d) support member States in the adoption and implementation of polices that protect the rights of young people and older persons.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

22.11	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. In particular, the tables of deliverables reflect updated deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026. For example, starting in 2026, ESCWA has scaled back its work on poverty and social justice within subprogramme 2, logistics, transport and trade facilitation within subprogramme 3, and risk assessment and conflict prevention within subprogramme 6. In addition, given that ESCWA has discontinued its work on trade data within subprogramme 4 activities related to the production of external trade statistics under result 2 of subprogramme 4 have been discontinued. These reflect mainly the identification of activities for which specialized agencies and other United Nations entities have the comparative advantage in implementation.

22.12	ESCWA will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, any decisions concerning proposals for strengthening collaboration with the United Nations Conference on Trade and Development, the Department of Economic and Social Affairs and the other regional commissions through joint mechanisms. ESCWA will jointly explore options for strengthening macroeconomic analysis capacities, with a view to contributing more harmonized analysis and policy support to Member States.

Evaluation activities
22.15	The following evaluations, completed by ESCWA in 2025, have guided the programme plan for 2027:
(a)	Evaluation of the ESCWA Stories project;
(b)	Evaluation of a project on reducing road deaths and injuries.

22.16	In response to the results of the evaluations referenced above, the Commission: (a) will continue to engage member States in positioning the ESCWA Stories model within broader education for sustainable development and will translate the lessons learned into programmatic delivery across areas of work; and (b) will further enhance road safety interventions by expanding outreach to civil society actors and ensuring strong multi-stakeholder coordination. The evaluation of ESCWA reform and its new operational model planned for 2025 was not completed owing to liquidity-related constraints.
22.17	An evaluation of one of the six ESCWA subprogrammes is planned for 2027.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Climate change and natural resource sustainability
Objective
22.18	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance climate action and integrated and sustainable policies in the areas of water, energy and food security.
Strategy
22.19	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Deploy in-house expertise and collaborate with other United Nations entities and external experts to analyse and assess the impact of trends and challenges, and disseminate knowledge products pertaining to issues such as transboundary and the integrated management of water resources; land degradation; critical energy transition minerals; equitable, resilient, inclusive and sustainable food and energy systems; circular economy approaches; sand and dust storms; climate adaptation and mitigation and the means for assessing climate finance and data-driven knowledge platforms related to climate resilience;

(b)	Provide technical and policy advice and expertise to member States and strengthen capacities at the regional, national and sectoral levels to use evidence and tools to identify and operationalize solutions and mainstream considerations related to climate change, sustainable natural resource management and food security into national development plans and budgets, and identify opportunities for financing climate action and adaptation;

(c)	Convene intergovernmental platforms and technical bodies to facilitate cross-sectoral dialogue, consensus-building and peer-to-peer exchanges among policymakers, practitioners, experts, civil society and other development partners from within and outside the region to promote cooperation and coordination; foster consensus towards the development of national, transboundary and regional normative frameworks on the sustainable management of water, energy and land resources; and increase access to affordable, reliable and modern energy;

(d)	Advocate with national Governments, regional intergovernmental bodies and relevant stakeholders, including through supporting integrated, regional reporting on and monitoring of progress against specific Sustainable Development Goal targets; promote joint solutions and the harmonization of positions; and leverage partnerships in support of the implementation and financing of such commitments.
22.20	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Adoption by member States of national policy frameworks and regional agreements related to climate action and the sustainable use and management of natural resources; reduced emissions; the transition away from fossil fuels in energy systems in a just, orderly and equitable manner; resilient food systems supported by sustainable agriculture; and improved climate resilience;
(b)	Strengthened cooperation on issues outlined in paragraph 22.19 (a), supported by inclusive networks and participatory coordination mechanisms.
Programme performance in 2025
Arab least developed countries make the transition to affordable, reliable, sustainable and modern energy supported by the deployment of risk-mitigating financial and business model tools

22.21	As identified in the Doha Programme of Action, the least developed countries in the Arab region continue to face barriers to energy access, including a persistent lack of access to affordable, reliable, sustainable and modern energy, low electrification rates, a reliance on traditional biomass systems and limited access to finance. To address these barriers, the subprogramme, together with regional and international partners, developed financial instruments, business models and digital toolkits to mitigate risks and promote scalable energy solutions. Member States benefited from knowledge products on financing approaches and project models for small-scale initiatives, while policy options and investment frameworks were prepared to guide the effective use of financial instruments and to mobilize capital for sustainable energy projects. Those efforts were further strengthened through regional workshops and capacity-building activities. This enabled officials and local actors, including rural women entrepreneurs, to apply microfinance, cost-accounting and project-development tools.

children.text: Result 3: improved resilience of surface water and groundwater basins in the Arab region – Proposed programme plan for 2027

22.27	Across the Arab region, climate change is contributing to a reduction in freshwater resources, prompting member States to strengthen basin resilience through targeted interventions supported by regional cooperation and policy frameworks. The subprogramme has built on the development of country-specific, climate-resilient watershed management packages in Algeria and Lebanon, laying the groundwork for basin initiatives integrating climate action.
Lessons learned and planned change

22.28	The lesson for the subprogramme is that its support for building resilience in surface water and groundwater basins was most effective when grounded in science-based assessments and combined with technical assistance to inform enabling policies to create conditions for local interventions. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will expand pilots to additional member States by systematically anchoring future interventions in science-based assessments that are adapted to local contexts and using evidence from implementation to guide policy tools for mainstreaming climate action into water governance.

Subprogramme 2 – Gender justice, population and inclusive development
Objective

22.31	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to achieve equitable and inclusive social development and reduced inequality, poverty and unemployment in line with the principle of leaving no one behind.
Strategy
22.32	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Develop knowledge products and policy options and solutions, underpinned by the analysis of trends and challenges, international norms and regional perspectives on a wide range of issues pertaining to socioeconomic equality and social protection; social and gender justice; poverty reduction; labour market needs; migration; and the socioeconomic participation and empowerment of women, young people, older persons and persons with disabilities, especially those in vulnerable situations;

(b)	Support mandated reviews against global commitments and agreements; provide technical and policy advice; and strengthen national capacities to assess existing policy and legislative frameworks to identify and operationalize viable legislation and solutions and to mainstream the reduction in inequalities and equal participation, protection and specific needs of those in vulnerable situations into national development plans and budgets;

(c)	Enhance existing and develop new methodologies, regional public goods and interactive tools for the simulation and analysis of policy options and their impact on inclusive development trajectories;

(d)	Engage member States and development stakeholders in dialogue to advocate for the adoption of policy solutions and to forge consensus on issues outlined in paragraph 22.32 (a) above, while promoting South-South (intraregional and interregional) collaboration and peer learning.
22.33	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	National policy frameworks and strategies adopted by member States to address structural inequalities and promote social justice and the greater social inclusion and socioeconomic empowerment and participation of women, young people, older persons, persons with disabilities and migrants, especially those in vulnerable situations;

(b)	Enhanced coverage of peoples of the region, in particular persons in vulnerable situations, through social protection and poverty eradication programmes, and their improved access to decent and equitable employment opportunities.
Programme performance in 2025
Young people in the Arab region are connected with employment opportunities

22.34	Arab labour markets face growing pressure as increasing numbers of educated young people enter the workforce amid an often-limited expansion of formal employment, leading to many young people entering low-paid or informal employment. To support the creation of an enabling environment that enhances the access of young people to decent employment, the subprogramme partnered with the Governments of Egypt, Jordan and Lebanon, 7 Arab universities and more than 30 private sector entities to launch an initiative targeting policymakers, businesses and young people. The subprogramme brought together all stakeholders to discuss labour market policies and presented policy recommendations. Young people were provided with free training in areas where there was a mismatch between skills available and skills needed in the labour market, in order to meet private sector labour demand, and were provided with private sector employment opportunities. Policymakers received tailored policy advice and recommendations and participated in South-South exchanges, and the private sector benefited from guidance on growth and youth employment strategies.

children.text: Result 3: member States identify and initiate priority action for the economic empowerment of persons with disabilities – Proposed programme plan for 2027

22.40	Formal employment opportunities remain limited in many parts of the Arab region, with people in vulnerable situations, including persons with disability, facing disproportionate barriers to gaining access to decent work The subprogramme collaborated with the International Labour Organization to equip member States with regional frameworks and modules on inclusive employment, produced a national situational analysis of disability for 18 member States and supported the development of national strategies for the economic empowerment of persons with disabilities.
Lessons learned and planned change

22.41	The lesson for the subprogramme was that the involvement of the private sector and organizations of persons with disabilities in the identification and implementation of priority action for the economic empowerment of persons with disabilities could enhance the relevance and sustainability of such action. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme, in partnership with the International Labour Organization, will launch the “Arab Inclusive Employment and Entrepreneurship Compact”, a tripartite commitment involving public and private actors and organizations of persons with disabilities and will develop a regional road map for policy action on the economic empowerment of persons with disabilities, building on global and regional success stories and best practices.

Subprogramme 3 – Shared economic prosperity
Objective

22.44	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to achieve equitable economic growth, amplify regional interconnectedness and integration, and advance the effective implementation of the Sevilla Commitment of the Fourth International Conference on Financing for Development in support of the 2030 Agenda.
Strategy
22.45	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Develop and provide knowledge products and evidence for policymaking related to international trade, logistics and transport, the application of strategic foresight in economic development, public policy monitoring and evaluation systems; economic diversification and macroeconomic stability; debt sustainability and fiscal policies, as a means of enhancing investment in sustainable development priorities; and financing for development, including integrated national financing frameworks;

(b)	Enhance existing and develop new dynamic, data-driven policy simulation tools (anchored in artificial intelligence and machine-learning approaches) pertaining to trade policy, budgeting, taxation, debt optimization, debt swaps, social expenditure, the monitoring and evaluation of development plans, and contextualized economic modelling; and promote the application of modern technologies to (i) facilitate financing of the Sustainable Development Goals and (ii) trade and logistics;

(c)	Make available to its member States policy advice and technical expertise and strengthen capacities to deploy strategic foresight and modelling and impact simulation tools in support of decision-making and policy development processes aimed at providing immediate, medium- and long-term solutions to national development challenges in the areas identified in paragraph 22.45(a) above;

(d)	Promote collaboration, coordination and the exchange of good practice, and foster consensus on priorities and joint initiatives aimed at improving connectivity in global value chains through export-led strategies, promoting innovative debt solutions and enhancing fiscal space and financing for sustainable development initiatives.
22.46	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Evidence-backed development planning; member States adopting policy frameworks, including those anchored in data-driven tools, to address structural economic challenges; improved debt management and sustainability; enhanced equity, efficiency and effectiveness of public spending and budgeting, including on national development priorities; new trade opportunities and economic diversification, including enhanced operational environments for small and medium-sized enterprises; and the creation of fiscal space for the financing of the Sustainable Development Goals and social investment in basic social services and sustainable development goals;

(b)	Increased contribution of small and medium-sized enterprises to national and regional economies, and greater economic participation and empowerment of individuals, including those in vulnerable situations.
Programme performance in 2025
Jordan and Tunisia developed macro-fiscal policies for enhanced social sector financing and fiscal sustainability

22.47	Allocating resources for social spending remains a challenge for many countries amid evolving global economic conditions and continued fiscal constraints. The subprogramme, in collaboration with the Department of Economic and Social Affairs, supported member States in strengthening their capacity to develop macro-fiscal policies to enhance social sector financing through macroeconometric modelling. This was done through a dynamic policy simulation tool that is built on the country-specific World Economic Forecasting Model and is customized to the national context. The tool provided a user-friendly interface to make policy simulations accessible to high-level policymakers and enabled targeted capacity-building, equipping officials from finance and planning ministries with the model, interface, user manuals and tailored policy recommendations and simulations needed for financing social sectors while maintaining fiscal sustainability.

children.text: Result 3: Arab countries conduct macroeconomic impact assessments for economic diversification – Proposed programme plan for 2027

22.53	The Arab region is exposed to external shocks, which can affect economic growth prospects across many Arab countries. The subprogramme has supported policymakers in evaluating scenarios, assessing trade-offs and designing policies for inclusive growth and macroeconomic stability. This was delivered through ex ante policy simulations, generated with an enhanced structural macroeconomic model and presented through an interactive tool.
Lessons learned and planned change

22.54	The lesson for the subprogramme was that its macroeconomic support tools needed to be sufficiently comprehensive and adaptable to help member States to assess the macroeconomic implications of policies related to public expenditure, taxation, economic diversification, sectoral development and exposure to global shocks. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will deliver national dynamic, country-specific impact assessment and scenario-planning tools and build the capacity of member States to evaluate economic scenarios and assess policy options, especially for diversification, thereby supporting the design of alternative strategies.

Subprogramme 4 – Statistics, the information society and technology
Objective

22.57	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to strengthen the development of official statistical frameworks, improve the quality and availability of statistics and advance the information society by accelerating the integration of technology and innovation for sustainable development in the Arab region.
Strategy
22.58	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Develop and promote methodologies and tools to enhance the capacities of national statistical systems to implement and adapt to evolving international statistical frameworks, including the 2025 System of National Accounts and digital-era statistical classifications, and promote the integration of innovative data sources and geospatial information and frontier technologies;

(b)	In line with global and regional processes, such as the Global Digital Compact, the World Summit on the Information Society+ 20 Forum High-Level Event 2025 and the Arab Digital Agenda for 2023–2033, develop knowledge products, models, toolkits and platforms, and enhance the foresight and analytical capacity of policymakers and practitioners in member States to use data and evidence in shaping and formulating digital development agendas, policy frameworks and strategies, for inclusive digital infrastructure, transformation, economy, government and accessibility, in support of the implementation of the 2030 Agenda;

(c)	Build national capacities to produce and utilize quality statistics and data for evidence-based policymaking and provide technical support for the development and adoption of modern policy frameworks, statistical methodologies and innovative solutions to monitor and report on sustainable development indicators, and increase the value created from data systems, geospatially enabled information and digital public infrastructure;

(d)	Convene intergovernmental and technical, multi-stakeholder forums to foster consensus and facilitate dialogue and exchanges, with the aim of promoting cooperation, partnerships and coordination and incentivizing investment in advancing both the digital development and modernization of official statistics.
22.59	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Enhanced value chain of timely, accessible, interoperable and integrated data from diverse sources, including administrative, geospatial and disaggregated data, that is compliant with international statistical standards and governance principles;

(b)	Member States developing, adopting and implementing forward-looking statistical and digital policy frameworks harnessing digital and emerging technologies, as a means of accelerating the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened national accounts in the Arab region for evidence-based policymaking

22.60	Robust and reliable national accounts support informed, evidence-based policymaking and sound economic management in all countries, including in countries affected by armed conflict where data gaps may hinder recovery planning and investment decisions. The subprogramme supported Jordan and the Syrian Arab Republic in aligning their national accounts with the 2008 System of National Accounts, to help them to build institutional capacities, enhancing methodological rigour and ensuring international comparability. In the Syrian Arab Republic, the subprogramme coordinated with the planning and statistics authority and national stakeholders to compile gross domestic product data across the 17 core economic activities, in accordance with international statistical standards for national accounts, thereby restoring the country’s ability to produce internationally comparable macroeconomic statistics for the first time in recent years, enabling policymakers to ground recovery strategies and policy dialogue in reliable evidence. In Jordan, the subprogramme enhanced national accounts capacity by expanding coverage to priority areas, including the non-formal sector.

children.text: Result 3: policymakers use the integrated regional data platform for evidence-based policymaking – Proposed programme plan for 2027

22.66	The adoption of modernized statistical systems and integrated regional data can help to meet today’s complex and fast-changing policy needs. By leveraging digital technologies, artificial intelligence and interoperable data platforms, countries can generate timely and comparable statistics to monitor progress made in achieving the Sustainable Development Goals and address cross-border challenges such as climate change, migration and regional economic integration. In 2024, the Arab Development Portal was upgraded to harmonize statistical methodologies, integrate alternative data sources and link data with policy analysis through artificial intelligence-enabled data-processing and interoperability layers, enabling the subprogramme to support member States in enhancing statistical systems and integrating alternative data sources and digital solutions in order to support Goal monitoring and inform policymaking.
Lessons learned and planned change

22.67	The lesson for the subprogramme was that addressing individual statistical gaps in isolation might limit the ability of member States to generate integrated evidence for policymaking. In applying the lesson, and building on the 2024 upgrade, the subprogramme will further enhance the Arab Development Portal, transforming it into a regional data-policy integration system by embedding artificial intelligence-driven predictive analytics, geospatial modelling and policy scenario simulation tools to support interactive and forward-looking analysis. It will also strengthen member States’ capacity to generate disaggregated indicators and integrate alternative and big data sources into policy design and impact assessments using shared regional data frameworks, thereby promoting coordination and harmonization for comparable, policy-relevant data and statistics.

Subprogramme 5 – 2030 Agenda and SDG coordination
Objective

22.70	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to accelerate progress towards sustainable development in the Arab region in line with the 2030 Agenda and to advance intraregional collaboration and a multi-stakeholder approach to key regional and subregional sustainable development issues.
Strategy
22.71	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Advocate and partner with a diverse set of stakeholders, including policymakers, the private sector, academic institutions and civil society, for principled action towards the implementation of the 2030 Agenda, in line with the commitment to “leave no one behind” and a people-centred approach to development, in order to promote a whole-of-government and whole-of-society approach;

(b)	Develop and disseminate knowledge products underpinned by the analysis of member States’ progress towards the implementation of the 2030 Agenda, including gaps in policy frameworks, and promote transformative development solutions for accelerating inclusive, sustainable development in the region;

(c)	Support the five Arab least developed countries, within the framework of the Doha Programme of Action for the Least Developed Countries, by strengthening their institutional capacities for implementing the 2030 Agenda and graduating from the least developed countries category;

(d)	Enhance capacities to conduct integrated monitoring and reporting, at the local, national and regional levels, of the progress against specific Sustainable Development Goals and targets, including through building national and institutional capacity to conduct voluntary national reviews and the identification of policy gaps; identify and disseminate innovative policy solutions and recommendations; ensure that the region’s perspective is reflected through the global lens; and foster cross-sectoral coordination of the Commission’s support to member States’ efforts to achieve the Goals;

(e)	Convene, and ensure ongoing support to and follow-up on various platforms, in connection with the Arab Forum for Sustainable Development, to facilitate multi-stakeholder engagement and dialogue and peer-to-peer exchanges; to foster consensus on sustainable development priorities and action, including for a global development agenda beyond 2030; to promote regional (South-South) and triangular collaboration and coordination; and to support cross-sectoral cooperation and the alignment of national development priorities and plans with both the 2030 Agenda and resources available for their implementation.
22.72	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Enhanced commitment of policymakers and practitioners, regional intergovernmental organizations, the private sector, including financial institutions; academia and think tanks, and civil society organizations in the Arab region to the principles of the 2030 Agenda;

(b)	Adoption by member States and development stakeholders of a more participatory, whole-of-government and whole-of-society approach to the implementation of and follow-up on Sustainable Development Goal priorities and related actions;

(c)	Improved utilization by member States and development stakeholders of available dialogue and advocacy platforms as a means of promoting consensus, peer-to-peer learning and coordination at the national, regional and global levels with respect to sustainable development priorities and action, including for a global development agenda beyond 2030; and mobilizing sustained, targeted financing for an accelerated implementation of the 2030 Agenda;

(d)	Improved capacity of policymakers and other stakeholders to identify policy gaps and related policy opportunities and solutions and take action to advance the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals.
Programme performance in 2025
Member States agree on a shared position to negotiate the political declaration of the Second World Summit for Social Development

22.73	The Second World Summit for Social Development was a platform to renew the commitments made under the Copenhagen Declaration on Social Development and to inject new momentum into the implementation of the 2030 Agenda. The subprogramme, in collaboration with the other regional economic commissions, analysed social development progress and challenges across regions and identified policy lessons to inform the next phase of action. To strengthen the engagement and negotiation capacity of member States, the subprogramme prepared a policy brief and a set of key messages outlining priority actions required to achieve inclusive social development amid armed conflict, climate change, fiscal constraints and evolving demographic, environmental, technological and labour market trends. Concurrently, it facilitated regional and subregional dialogues with governments – including ministries of labour, social affairs and foreign affairs – and other stakeholders and collaborated with the League of Arab States to facilitate consensus at the ministerial level.

children.text: Result 3: a regional outlook in place for identifying medium- to long-term regional development pathways – Proposed programme plan for 2027

22.79	The formulation of medium- to long-term regional development priorities and plans provides an important avenue to address persistent developments challenges and close existing gaps in implementation. The subprogramme analysed the region’s progress in advancing internationally agreed development frameworks and commitments, reviewed national policy landscapes and drew policy lessons. It also facilitated a dialogue on effective regional and international cooperation affecting global action on shared development problems.
Lessons learned and planned change

22.80	The lesson for the subprogramme was that it needed to facilitate member State agreement on a shared set of guidelines to enable a more invigorated, targeted and implementation-oriented approach to sustainable development in the medium to long term. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will establish an Arab advisory board, composed of government, think tank and civil society representatives, to develop a set of guidelines to help to shape the region’s common medium- to long-term priorities, aspirations and needs for sustainable development. It will also provide member States with the knowledge base and multi-stakeholder dialogue platforms needed to identify and articulate the areas in which regional coordination and cooperation would maximize the benefits of international mechanisms for member States and the region.

Subprogramme 6 – Governance and conflict prevention
Objective

22.83	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance just, peaceful and inclusive societies, especially for people living under occupation or in conflict or post-conflict settings; to improve governance and strengthen effective, efficient and accountable public institutions; and to enhance service delivery.
Strategy
22.84	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Generate and make available evidence-based and foresight-driven policy analysis and recommendations to member States and relevant stakeholders, grounded in the principles of a human development approach, with a focus on mitigating the impact of conflict and occupation on the capacity to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals, facilitating national recovery and reconciliation and improving governance and institutional capacities to deliver basic services, anticipate risks and respond to shocks; strengthening regional cooperation on governance and public administration; and boosting competitiveness, including consumer protection and fair market mechanisms;

(b)	Provide technical expertise, methodologies and digital tools while enhancing capacities to identify and operationalize immediate, medium- and long-term solutions, aimed at pre-emptively addressing emerging socioeconomic, environmental and governance challenges and conflicts; enhancing prevention, recovery and development planning; improving transparency, accountability and accessibility in service delivery and the efficiency of State-run services; and reinforcing competition policies and consumer protection frameworks to foster trust and inclusive growth;

(c)	Foster South-South (intraregional and interregional) and triangular cooperation, including through foresight and early warning mechanisms, and build consensus on priorities and action required to minimize the impact of conflict and any sudden shocks, inter alia, on the ability of member States to advance their national development agendas;

(d)	Support the five Arab least developed countries, within the framework of the Doha Programme of Action (2022–2031), by strengthening their institutional capacities for implementing the 2030 Agenda and graduating from the least developed countries category;

(e)	Enhance the capabilities of Palestinian institutions to mitigate the adverse effects of the Israeli occupation on development, including supporting national recovery and resilience efforts and capacities in the Occupied Palestinian Territory and reducing the asymmetric dependency of its economy.
22.85	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Improved understanding and prevention of development challenges associated with sudden shocks, including conflict, and more effective mitigation of their impact on national development, with the aim of strengthening recovery pathways and enhancing national resilience;

(b)	More effective, transparent and accountable public institutions, in particular in the five Arab least developed countries, with enhanced capabilities to deliver basic services in a transparent, accessible and inclusive way through modernized and effective public administration systems;
(c)	Operationalization of a contextualized, people-centred approach to development planning and policy-formulation;
(d)	A more competitive and transparent business environment and more effective consumer protection mechanisms.
Programme performance in 2025
Oman employs evidence-based local development planning

22.86	Oman Vision 2040 provides a blueprint to help Oman to remain competitive and further diversify its economy supporting innovation-led, balanced and decentralized development with equitable growth across all governorates. The subprogramme supported the Sultanate of Oman in developing and institutionalizing the governorate competitiveness index as a strategic tool for local development planning. In close collaboration with the Ministry of Economy in Oman, the subprogramme designed the index framework, consolidating it into 4 themes, 13 pillars and 61 indicators. Broad national ownership was secured through a series of consultative meetings with ministries, governorates and private sector representatives, embedding a participatory approach into the process.

children.text: Result 3: strengthened competition governance for transparent and inclusive markets – Proposed programme plan for 2027

22.92	While fair and transparent markets can support inclusive growth, competition frameworks in the Arab region continue to evolve, with varying levels of enforcement capacity, institutional mandates and specialized expertise. The subprogramme produced an updated Arab Business Legislative Framework, a regional benchmark of competition laws that offers recommendations on competition legislative reforms, and engaged member States in a regional dialogue facilitating coordination and collaboration at the national, regional and international levels.
Lessons learned and planned change

22.93	The lesson for the subprogramme was that adopting competition laws represented only a first step and that its support was more impactful when it also addressed enforcement and implementation capacities in line with national contexts. Enforcement also benefits from its application at both the institutional and sectoral levels to ensure that markets function fairly. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will support member States in developing sector-specific strategies for implementing competition law and in strengthening the enforcement of the law at all levels and across sectors.
